Scenario.title: Operation Ghost: Targeted Disruption of Energy Infrastructure
Scenario.overview: Energy Intermediate This scenario details how Operation Ghost infiltrated an energy provider through spearphishing, exploited internal APIs, and manipulated industrial control systems to cause grid instability.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: ["Employee Email", "SSL Certificates and Attribution", "Custom APIs", "Industrial Control Systems", "Obsolete Products", "Shared Directory"]

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: Attackers sent highly targeted emails containing malicious attachments disguised as SCADA system update documents to energy sector employees, leveraging social engineering to bypass awareness training.

stage.name: Execution
stage.description: A payload embedded in the phishing attachment exploited a zero-day vulnerability in the victim's energy management API, allowing arbitrary code execution within the industrial network perimeter.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: Malware created scheduled tasks in shared engineering directories to periodically dump credentials from local security databases, exploiting weak directory permissions common in OT environments.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.technique_name: Exploit Default Credentials on RTU Devices
stage.description: Attackers leveraged hardcoded credentials on legacy RTUs (e.g., Modicon PLCs) to gain administrative access to supervisory control systems through unsecured serial ports.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.capec: CAPEC-636: Hiding Malicious Data or Code within Files
stage.description: Command-and-control traffic was hidden within encrypted telemetry data streams using LSB steganography, making detection difficult amid normal grid monitoring activity.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: Custom malware injected false sensor readings into the grid's frequency regulation system, causing cascading outages by triggering automatic load shedding mechanisms.

Scenario.summary: Operation Ghost executed a multi-stage attack against an energy provider, beginning with spearphishing to establish initial access. Through API exploitation and lateral movement via legacy devices, attackers ultimately disrupted grid operations by manipulating industrial control systems. The campaign demonstrated advanced knowledge of both IT and operational technology environments.
